planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 7–9; Princess Persimmon is a small, multi-stemmed deciduous tree or large shrub from coastal woodlands of eastern China (Zhejiang, Fujian, Jiangsu) — grown primarily as an ornamental for its spectacular autumn display of tiny, bright orange-red persimmon fruits massed on bare or nearly bare branches, with excellent container and bonsai potential, and foliage that turns vivid red-orange in autumn.

directions: Plant Princess Persimmon in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly during establishment; minimal pruning needed — mainly to shape; the tiny fruits persist through winter and are best left for ornamental effect and wildlife. Diospyros rhombifolia is a gem of the East Asian ornamental plant palette — a compact, slow-growing tree or large multi-stemmed shrub that transforms dramatically in autumn. The tiny round persimmon fruits (barely 0.5 inch across) ripen to vivid orange-red and are produced in such abundance that entire branches become festooned with bright baubles, creating one of the most spectacular ornamental displays of any small tree in the autumn and early winter garden. The fruits are technically edible once fully ripe and soft — very sweet, but seedy and small — though most gardeners leave them for wildlife and ornamental effect. Autumn foliage also turns attractive shades of red-orange before leaf drop, leaving the brilliant fruit display on bare branches well into winter. An excellent subject for container growing, bonsai, and small garden spaces where a larger persimmon tree would be impractical. Dioecious (male and female plants separate) — fruiting plants are female; ensure at least one male plant is within pollination range for reliable fruit set.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in deep, fertile, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Monkey Jack (Lakuch, Barhal, Monkey Fruit) is a medium to large evergreen tree of the Moraceae family — related to Jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus) and Breadfruit (A. altilis) — native to humid tropical forests from India and Sri Lanka through Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia, producing round to irregularly lobed, golden-yellow to reddish composite fruits with sweet-sour, aromatic, slightly astringent yellow flesh, widely consumed fresh and in regional cooking throughout South and Southeast Asia.

directions: Plant Monkey Jack in deep, fertile, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun in zone 10–12; water well during establishment and dry seasons; harvest fruits when they turn golden-yellow and yield to gentle pressure — use gloves as the latex sap stains skin and clothing. Artocarpus lacucha produces round to irregularly shaped compound fruits 3–6 inches across — structurally similar to small jackfruits, formed from fused fruitlets on a central core — with golden-yellow to orange-red skin studded with small bumps and sweet-sour, aromatic, slightly astringent yellow flesh. The flavor is described as a complex blend of mango, pineapple, and slightly acidic tropical notes; the fruits are eaten fresh, pickled, cooked in curries, and used in chutneys and preserves throughout its native range. The starchy seeds are also edible when boiled or roasted, similar to jackfruit seeds. Various parts of the tree are used in traditional Ayurvedic and Thai medicine — the bark yields a yellow dye used for dyeing Buddhist monk robes in Thailand and Myanmar. A productive and ornamentally handsome tree for large tropical gardens, with attractive compound leaves and interesting fruits hanging from the main trunk and larger branches.
sources: Orwa, C. et al. — Agroforestree Database

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in deep, fertile, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Galip Nut (Canarium Nut, Pili relative, Nuss) is a large evergreen timber and nut tree of the Burseraceae family native to the humid lowland forests of Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, and adjacent Melanesia — the most economically important indigenous nut tree of Papua New Guinea — producing clusters of dark purple, olive-like drupes enclosing hard-shelled nuts with rich, sweet, high-fat kernels eaten fresh, roasted, and processed for cooking oil.

directions: Plant Galip Nut in deep, fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun in zone 10–12; water regularly during establishment; large trees are fairly drought-tolerant when established; harvest nut clusters when the outer drupes turn dark purple-black. Canarium indicum produces clusters of dark purple, almond-sized drupes resembling large olives, each enclosing a very hard, triangular-ridged shell containing a single cream-colored kernel of exceptional quality. The kernels are rich (approximately 70% fat) with a sweet, mild, buttery flavor reminiscent of a combination of almonds and macadamia nuts — widely considered among the finest edible nuts of the Pacific region. They are eaten fresh directly from the shell, roasted over fire, and processed into oil for cooking and for use in traditional body oil preparations. Galip nuts are the single most important indigenous tree nut of Papua New Guinea, traded widely in coastal markets, and of growing international interest as a specialty nut crop. The large trees (reaching 60–80 feet in their native forest habitat) are also highly valued for their straight-grained, durable timber. Closely related to the Pili Nut (Canarium ovatum) of the Philippines, which is also commercially grown; both species deserve wider cultivation in tropical Pacific and Caribbean gardens.
sources: Leakey, R.R.B. et al. — 'Agroforestry for Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services' (2012)
FAO — Non-Wood Forest Products — Pacific Island edible nuts

subcategory: Southern African drought-tolerant edible tuber legume

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or in warm soil in full sun in sandy, well-drained, nutrient-poor soil in zone 9–12; Marama Bean (Morama Bean, Gemsbok Bean) is a perennial, tuberous-rooted wild legume from the Kalahari and Namib desert regions of Botswana, Namibia, and South Africa — a trailing vine developing an enormous edible storage tuber (sometimes reaching 100 kg in old plants) and producing flat woody pods with 2–3 large seeds with outstanding nutritional content: 30–35% protein and 40% fat, comparable to soybeans and peanuts combined.

directions: Direct sow Marama Bean in sandy, well-drained, nutrient-poor soil in full sun after last frost or year-round in zone 10–12; minimal fertilization needed; plants develop a large taproot tuber — do not disturb; harvest seeds when pods dry; water only during establishment. Tylosema esculentum is one of the most nutritionally remarkable wild legumes known — its seeds contain approximately 30–35% protein and 38–40% fat, making them nutritionally comparable to combining soybean and peanut in a single seed. For the San (Bushman) and other indigenous peoples of the Kalahari, marama beans have been a critical dry-season protein and fat source for thousands of years — seeds are boiled, roasted, or ground into a nutritious paste or flour, and the seeds can also be roasted to produce a beverage resembling coffee. The massive underground tuber (technically a massive swollen hypocotyl) accumulates water and carbohydrates over decades — older plants may develop tubers weighing 50–100 kg — and the water-rich flesh has historically provided emergency moisture in the desert. The trailing stems and attractive round leaves grow across sandy desert soils and fix nitrogen through root nodules. Under cultivation in well-drained, sunny garden conditions in zone 9–12, marama bean provides a drought-tolerant ground cover and nitrogen fixer while producing protein-rich seeds. Of serious interest to researchers as a potential drought-tolerant protein crop for arid regions facing food insecurity.
sources: Mojeremane, W. & Tshwenyane, S.O. — 'Marama bean: A multipurpose legume of Botswana', Pakistan Journal of Nutrition (2004)
National Research Council — 'Lost Crops of Africa Vol. 2: Vegetables' (2006)

subcategory: Brazilian Atlantic Forest myrtaceous berry tree

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade to full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained, acidic soil in zone 9–11; Cabeludinha (Hairy Jaboticaba, Peluda) is a small to medium fruiting tree in the Myrtaceae family from the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil (Rio de Janeiro, Minas Gerais, São Paulo) — closely related to the jaboticaba complex — producing clusters of small, round, yellowish-orange to golden fruits covered in short, fine hairs, with sweet-tart, grape-like translucent pulp used for fresh eating, juices, and liqueurs in the region.

directions: Plant Cabeludinha in fertile, moist, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; water consistently; maintain high organic matter; protect from hard frost below 28°F; harvest fruits when fully colored and give slightly to gentle pressure. Myrciaria glazioviana is one of the lesser-known but highly regarded wild fruits of Brazil's endangered Atlantic Forest — a small to medium-sized tree whose golden, hairy fruits are prized locally though almost unknown internationally. The fruits (roughly 0.5–1 inch diameter) are covered in short, fine hairs that give them a distinctly fuzzy appearance, unlike the smooth skin of the closely related jaboticaba (Myrciaria cauliflora). The pulp is translucent, juicy, sweet-tart, and grape-like in texture — excellent eaten fresh directly from the tree and used for juices, fruit wines, and liqueurs in the same tradition as jaboticaba. Unlike jaboticaba, Cabeludinha produces its fruits on the branch tips rather than cauliflorous directly on the trunk, giving a different fruiting habit. Growing naturally in the understory to mid-canopy of Atlantic Forest remnants, it tolerates partial shade better than full-sun species. Part of the rich Myrtaceae fruit tree diversity from Brazil that includes Cambuci (Campomanesia phaea), Grumixama (Eugenia brasiliensis), and Araçá (Psidium cattleianum), representing an extraordinary regional flora of underexploited edible berries.
sources: Sobral, M. et al. — Flora of the State of Rio de Janeiro — Myrciaria glazioviana
Lorenzi, H., 'Brazilian Fruits and Cultivated Exotics' (2006)
